content:
I'm getting an error about Unknown symbol in module in a kernel-mode driver module I've written and was hoping someone might have an idea about what is going wrong and how to solve it.

Building the module works fine:

❯ make LLVM=1
mkdir -p build
make -C $HOME/Development/linux M=$HOME/Development/tt-kmd-rust MO=$HOME/Development/tt-kmd-rust/build LLVM=1 -j32
make[1]: Entering directory '$HOME/Development/linux'
make[2]: Entering directory '$HOME/Development/tt-kmd-rust/build'
  GEN     Makefile
  RUSTC [M] tt_core.o
  MODPOST Module.symvers
  CC [M]  tt_core.mod.o
  CC [M]  .module-common.o
  LD [M]  tt_core.ko
  BTF [M] tt_core.ko
make[2]: Leaving directory '$HOME/Development/tt-kmd-rust/build'

Loading the module with insmod gives this:

❯ sudo insmod --verbose ./build/tt_core.ko
insmod: ERROR: could not insert module ./build/tt_core.ko: Unknown symbol in module

Loading with modprobe shows the same thing:

❯ sudo modprobe --verbose ./build/tt_core.ko
insmod ./build/tt_core.ko
modprobe: ERROR: could not insert 'tt_core': Unknown symbol in module, or unknown parameter (see dmesg)

No additional output is shown on dmesg.

The error occurs only when I try to use code with kern_path:

let path = ::core::ptr::null_mut::<::kernel::bindings::path>();
let pathname = c"/sys/module/foo".as_ptr().cast();
let err = unsafe { ::kernel::bindings::kern_path(pathname, LOOKUP_FOLLOW, path) };
::kernel::error::to_result(err)

All the other code I've written for the driver module works fine and loads without issue.

I've modified rust/bindings/bindings_helper.h to include the header for kern_path:

#include <linux/namei.h>

This generates the binding:

extern "C" {
    pub fn kern_path(arg1: *const ffi::c_char, arg2: ffi::c_uint, arg3: *mut path) -> ffi::c_int;
}

I believe this should work given the corresponding C definitions:

// include/linux/namei.h
extern int kern_path(const char *, unsigned, struct path *);

// fs/namei.c
int kern_path(const char *name, unsigned int flags, struct path *path)
{
 struct filename *filename = getname_kernel(name);
 int ret = filename_lookup(AT_FDCWD, filename, flags, path, NULL);

 putname(filename);
 return ret;

}
EXPORT_SYMBOL(kern_path);

The symbol also seems to be available in the kernel:

❯ cat /proc/kallsyms | grep -i kern_path
0000000000000000 T __pfx_kern_path_locked_negative
0000000000000000 T kern_path_locked_negative
0000000000000000 T __pfx_kern_path_locked
0000000000000000 T kern_path_locked
0000000000000000 t __pfx___kern_path_locked
0000000000000000 t __kern_path_locked
0000000000000000 T __pfx_kern_path
0000000000000000 T kern_path
0000000000000000 T __pfx_kern_path_create
0000000000000000 T kern_path_create
0000000000000000 r __ksymtab_kern_path
0000000000000000 r __ksymtab_kern_path_create

Any ideas what the problem could be? Am I missing a step somewhere for generating the bindings?

I've added other bindings and they seemed to work, but they were also from imports already mentioned in rust/bindings/bindings_helper.h.
Sounds like you did it right to me. Can you use a debugger to figure out what symbol it is failing on?
You can also list the symbols in your module and check that all looks good.

Thanks for the response.

I did check the objdump previously but here it is for reference (with kern_path added back in):

❯ objdump -t build/tt_core.ko| rustfilt | grep UND
0000000000000000         *UND*  0000000000000000 <kernel::device::Device as kernel::types::AlwaysRefCounted>::dec_ref
0000000000000000         *UND*  0000000000000000 __x86_return_thunk
0000000000000000         *UND*  0000000000000000 rust_helper_pci_set_drvdata
0000000000000000         *UND*  0000000000000000 core::panicking::panic_bounds_check
0000000000000000         *UND*  0000000000000000 rust_helper_pci_get_drvdata
0000000000000000         *UND*  0000000000000000 misc_deregister
0000000000000000         *UND*  0000000000000000 core::fmt::pointer_fmt_inner
0000000000000000         *UND*  0000000000000000 <kernel::str::CStr as core::fmt::Display>::fmt
0000000000000000         *UND*  0000000000000000 <str as core::fmt::Display>::fmt
0000000000000000         *UND*  0000000000000000 kern_path
0000000000000000         *UND*  0000000000000000 filp_open
0000000000000000         *UND*  0000000000000000 kernel::error::to_result
0000000000000000         *UND*  0000000000000000 kernel::print::format_strings::INFO
0000000000000000         *UND*  0000000000000000 kernel::print::call_printk
0000000000000000         *UND*  0000000000000000 kobject_create_and_add
0000000000000000         *UND*  0000000000000000 filp_close
0000000000000000         *UND*  0000000000000000 kobject_del
0000000000000000         *UND*  0000000000000000 <usize as core::fmt::Display>::fmt
0000000000000000         *UND*  0000000000000000 rust_helper_atomic_read_acquire
0000000000000000         *UND*  0000000000000000 rust_helper_xa_lock
0000000000000000         *UND*  0000000000000000 __xa_erase
0000000000000000         *UND*  0000000000000000 rust_helper_xa_unlock
0000000000000000         *UND*  0000000000000000 <kernel::str::CString>::try_from_fmt
0000000000000000         *UND*  0000000000000000 core::str::converts::from_utf8
0000000000000000         *UND*  0000000000000000 <u16 as core::fmt::UpperHex>::fmt
0000000000000000         *UND*  0000000000000000 <kernel::pci::Device<kernel::device::Core>>::enable_device
0000000000000000         *UND*  0000000000000000 <kernel::pci::Device<kernel::device::Core>>::set_master
0000000000000000         *UND*  0000000000000000 rust_helper_xa_init_flags
0000000000000000         *UND*  0000000000000000 rust_helper_atomic_try_cmpxchg_relaxed
0000000000000000         *UND*  0000000000000000 rust_helper_atomic_set_release
0000000000000000         *UND*  0000000000000000 xa_find
0000000000000000         *UND*  0000000000000000 xa_find_after
0000000000000000         *UND*  0000000000000000 xa_destroy
0000000000000000         *UND*  0000000000000000 __xa_alloc
0000000000000000         *UND*  0000000000000000 <kernel::error::Error>::from_errno
0000000000000000         *UND*  0000000000000000 rust_helper_xa_zero_entry
0000000000000000         *UND*  0000000000000000 __xa_cmpxchg_raw
0000000000000000         *UND*  0000000000000000 rust_helper_xa_err
0000000000000000         *UND*  0000000000000000 kernel::print::format_strings::ERR
0000000000000000         *UND*  0000000000000000 xa_load
0000000000000000         *UND*  0000000000000000 strlen
0000000000000000         *UND*  0000000000000000 misc_register
0000000000000000         *UND*  0000000000000000 core::panicking::panic_const::panic_const_add_overflow
0000000000000000         *UND*  0000000000000000 core::panicking::panic_explicit
0000000000000000         *UND*  0000000000000000 <bindings::bindings_raw::pci_driver as core::default::Default>::default
0000000000000000         *UND*  0000000000000000 memcpy
0000000000000000         *UND*  0000000000000000 __pci_register_driver
0000000000000000         *UND*  0000000000000000 pci_unregister_driver
0000000000000000         *UND*  0000000000000000 kernel::module_param::PARAM_OPS_U8
0000000000000000         *UND*  0000000000000000 kernel::module_param::PARAM_OPS_U32
0000000000000000         *UND*  0000000000000000 core::panicking::assert_failed_inner
0000000000000000         *UND*  0000000000000000 generic_file_open
0000000000000000         *UND*  0000000000000000 <kernel::device::Device as kernel::types::AlwaysRefCounted>::inc_ref
0000000000000000         *UND*  0000000000000000 _dev_printk
0000000000000000         *UND*  0000000000000000 rust_helper_krealloc
0000000000000000         *UND*  0000000000000000 <kernel::sync::lock::mutex::MutexBackend as kernel::sync::lock::Backend>::init

I think that looks okay?

I ended up working around the problem by using filp_open and filp_close instead (as you can see from the symbols), although that does more than I want since I only care about the file existence. Still no idea why kern_path doesn't work.

I did try to debug with kdb but was unsuccessful in getting that set up correctly.

Whenever I try to drop to the kdb prompt on this system with SysRq-g, the system just hangs, or I might see the DEBUG msg just before the prompt should appear, and then it hangs. That is with kgdboc=kms,kbd.

Unfortunately I don't have a serial port on this system so my other options are limited.

I did try to get xdbc working, and although I apparently have some Debug capable USB ports on a few of the xhcis, I can't get the dbc entries to show up under sysfs to toggle them into debug mode, and I didn't have much luck getting it to automatically connect to a host with earlyprintk=xdbc either. This is an an AMD X870E Zen 5 system and mostly I've only seen people report success with that on Intel, so I don't know.

I also considered kgdboe but it only just added support for 6.14 I think, and I need some features from 6.16 so that makes it difficult to use in general, although maybe I could give it a try just for this isolated case.

I might be able to just run a QEMU instance with vfio-pci and pass through the card that this I'm writing this driver module for though, and debug through a virtual serial that way. I'll try that next probably.

I'm new to kernel programming and haven't figured out a good solution for debugging the live kernel with my current set up yet. If you have any suggestions for other things I could try I would appreciate that.
Oh, I always use qemu with the gdb stub. If it is a PCI device you are working with, you should be able to pass it through. I did that with NVMe devices in the past.
If you do objdump -t vmlinux | grep kern_path, do you get a hit?

Andreas Hindborg said:

If you do objdump -t vmlinux | grep kern_path, do you get a hit?

Yes, seems to be there:

❯ objdump -t src/linux/vmlinux | grep -i kern_path
ffffffff81735eb0 l     F .text  0000000000000134 __kern_path_locked
ffffffff81735ea0 l     F .text  0000000000000010 __pfx___kern_path_locked
ffffffff82f7c725 l       __ksymtab_strings      0000000000000000 __kstrtab_kern_path
ffffffff82f785ef l       __ksymtab_strings      0000000000000000 __kstrtabns_kern_path
ffffffff82f3d40c l       __ksymtab      0000000000000000 __ksymtab_kern_path
ffffffff82f91e68 l       __ksymtab_strings      0000000000000000 __kstrtab_kern_path_create
ffffffff82f785ef l       __ksymtab_strings      0000000000000000 __kstrtabns_kern_path_create
ffffffff82f3d418 l       __ksymtab      0000000000000000 __ksymtab_kern_path_create
ffffffff81735c40 g     F .text  0000000000000010 __pfx_kern_path_locked_negative
ffffffff81735df0 g     F .text  0000000000000010 __pfx_kern_path_locked
ffffffff817360a0 g     F .text  0000000000000010 __pfx_kern_path
ffffffff81738d00 g     F .text  0000000000000010 __pfx_kern_path_create
ffffffff81735c50 g     F .text  0000000000000192 kern_path_locked_negative
ffffffff81735e00 g     F .text  0000000000000094 kern_path_locked
ffffffff817360b0 g     F .text  000000000000009c kern_path
ffffffff81738d10 g     F .text  000000000000009b kern_path_create

I should also mention that I previously tried checking objdump output with the call to kern_path removed vs with it included and literally the only difference was kern_path.

It is possible maybe that some other function being called from kern_path is missing from the symbols? Not sure what else it could be, but if it is something like that, I'm also surprised it isn't handled by the build system.
It sounds like some kind of bug to me. Did you try with a full clean build?

Yeah, tried with a clean build. Tried with a few different kernel configurations too, so far no luck.

The only other time I've gotten issues with missing symbols is when trying to use some statics that were for some reason included in the generated bindings, but not exported, like module_kset and module_ktype, but those do not have an associated EXPORT_SYMBOL and the compile step fails before I even get a chance to load the module.

I haven't tried debugging with QEMU yet but will give that a try soon.

I guess the only reason I haven't switched to using that yet is just not having a good workflow for it in mind. Do you happen to know of any resources that describe a good approach for that? I'm thinking just for quickly looping through recompiling the module (and maybe kernel), redeploying, restarting, maybe automatically running tests in some semi-automated fashion. Maybe it's just a matter of writing some scripts for that.

Would also be nice to understand how to fit miri (to the extent possible) and kunit tests into that picture. I haven't seen many examples of using tests in the rust-for-linux examples I've looked at.

I have never run miri, but as far as I understand it uses symbolic execution and does not rely on the target. I think it operates on the MIR level. I don't think you can use it on the kernel rust code, and I don't think it would help solve this issue anyway.

But rust doctests and #[test] tests are picked up by kunit. I think there is a kunit target in the makefile, but in my scripts I run kunit with

tools/testing/kunit/kunit.py exec --build_dir ${BUILD_DIR} --arch=$ARCH

You need to build the bzImage first.
You need to enable kunit. If you make everything you want to test built-in, the test are run on boot, and this script will give you some nice output.

For debugging in qemu, you would enable debug symbols and then run qemu with -s -S switches. I forget which is which, but one of them enables the gdb stub in qemu, the other one pauses and waits for gdb to connect before booting.

Andreas Hindborg said:

You need to enable kunit. If you make everything you want to test built-in, the test are run on boot, and this script will give you some nice output.

I think it will also exit with nonzero exit status if a test fail.
You also have selftests in tools/testing/selftests. I never tried to use that infrastructure though, and I don't think we have any rust specific things in there.

Anyway, if it is actually a bug in the module loader, you probably want to set a break point in the code that tries to resolve the symbols for the module that is being loaded. Then you can maybe figure out why it won't patch in the symbol you are missing.
